entry.title: Manager not updating in SuccessFactors Learning for one user
entry.content:
Hi all,
We’re seeing an issue with one specific user where the manager is not updating in SuccessFactors Learning, even though the manager change has been correctly updated and saved in Employee Central.

The manager is correct in the EC employee profile (screenshot 1).
In Learning Administration, the user record still shows the previous manager (screenshot 2).

The user is active and the manager relationship in EC is valid.

Has anyone encountered a scenario where a single user’s manager change does not flow to Learning?
Any ideas on what could prevent the update would be appreciated.
Thanks in advance.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation
entry.content:
Authors: Adam Csallokozi, David Domokos

  • Let’s first determine your environment
  • Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount
  • 1. Create the CF Subaccount
  • 2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount
  • 3. Enable Cloud Foundry
  • Verify the Destination Service
  • 1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys
  • 2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
  • 3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service
  • 4. Generate the Certificate viaDestination Service REST API
  • 5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate
  • Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)
  • Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP
  • Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:
  • Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:
  • Conclusion

Driven by an industry-wide change by Certification Authorities (see SAP KBA 3711618 – Client Authentication EKU Deprecation), newly procured publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates may omit the “Client Authentication” Extended Key Usage (EKU) starting May 2026 as CAs separate server and client roles. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication (two‑way SSL/mTLS) with SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration, or API Management, the client certificate must include the Client Authentication EKU; certificates lacking this EKU will not be accepted by the Integration Suite endpoints.

There are multiple ways to implement this requirement. In this article,  we will demonstrate one of the three supported approaches, including the technical steps needed to generate and use the client certificate in your integration scenarios.

Let’s first determine your environment 

Before starting the implementation, first determine which environment applies to your landscape. The steps in this guide differ depending on the environment used for generating and signing the client certificate:

  • If you are working on Cloud Foundry environment please start with Verify the Destination Service chapter

  • If you are working on Neo environment please start with the Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount chapter

 

Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount

ℹ️ Please note that all steps described in this section can be performed free of charge and do not involve any additional costs ℹ️

⚠️ Please be aware to perform the listed actions you need to have a Global Account Admin role or appropriate admin colleague is required⚠️

If you are working on Neo environment, first create a new, Cloud Foundry (CF) Subaccount. This is required to use the Destination service to sign certificate.

1. Create the CF Subaccount

Go to your Global Account and click on 'Create' then choose 'Subaccount'

2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount

 Please fill the required fields:

  • Display Name: Choose the desired name for your subaccount
  • Region: Choose the desired region for your subaccount

the rest of the fields will be filled automatically, after that click on 'Create'.

3. Enable Cloud Foundry

 

Once the Subaccount has been created, please open it. Click on “Enable Cloud Foundry” button. In the "Enable Cloud Foundry" popup window, input desired value, then press button "Create". Once see "Cloud Foundry Environment" section get populated with Cloud Foundry organization data, the Subaccount creation is successfully completed. 

ℹ️If you need more detailed guide to complete this step you can check our Help Page: Create a Subaccount ℹ️

Verify the Destination Service

In case you are running Cloud Integration / Integration Suite with CF environment, then please go to the CF subaccount where Cloud Integration / Integration Suite is subscribed. In case you are running Cloud Integration with NEO environment, then please go to the newly created CF subaccount as mentioned in chapter 'Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount'.  Then access  menu 'Entitlements' and confirm 'Destination Service' with service plan 'lite' is listed.

 

1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys

In BTP Cockpit, go to ‘Instances and Subscriptions’ on the left, then choose the ‘Create’ button in the top right corner

In the pop-up, set Service to Destination Service and Plan to lite. Select the Space where you have administrator permissions, then enter an Instance Name that follows your naming convention. Once the Create button is enabled, click Create.

⚠️When you enable Cloud Foundry (CF) for a new subaccount, only the org is created by default, no spaces are created. As a result, when you create a Destination Service instance, you may see a popup (as shown below) prompting you to create a space first ⚠️

  The instance is now created and visible on the Instances list.

Next, create a service key for the instance. Click the “lite” link to open the details panel on the right, navigate to the Service Keys section, and choose Create.

 

In the pop-up, enter a name for the Service Key, then click Create.

You should now see the newly created service key listed:

Finally, click View Credentials to display the generated credentials; you’ll use them in the next chapter.


 

2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)

⚠️Command line (CMD) should be opened directly in the folder where the .KEY and .CSR files should be saved + OpenSSL needs to be installed on the computer ⚠️

To generate the private key and the CSR signed by a newly generated private key use openSSL. Execute the following command:

openssl req -utf8 -nodes -sha256 -newkey rsa:3072 -keyout genExample.key -out genExample.csr

⚠️All CSR attributes (shown below) will be overwritten during generation, so avoid spending too much time configuring them ⚠️

The command generates genExample.key (private key). You won’t need it for the API steps, but keep it for later use (for example, when creating a .PFX). As a last step of this chapter, the generated .CSR file needs to be Base64‑encoded. This can be done (in case of Windows operating system) via Windows Powershell.

Open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing genExample.csr and run the command:

[Convert]::ToBase64String([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("genExample.csr"))

as a result the Base64 encoded value of the .CSR file will be displayed in the PowerShell window:

Please copy that value and save it because it will be needed in a later step, you can save it as simple text file in the same folder as the previously created files.

3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service

In this step the OAuth token will be generated that is needed for the certificate creation. To retrieve that OAuth token, either follow the API documentation, or download our predefined REST API collection from here. This collection will be used for the rest if the steps in the 4th and 5th chapters.

Open the “DestinationServiceTokenGeneration” request and populate its variables with the actual values from the credentials you created in the last step of Chapter 1 (Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys).

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)url (https://XXXX.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com)
clientID (in the Body section)clientid (sb-clone9ae33f841f774443XXXXXXXXXXXXX)
clientSecret (in the Body section)clientsecret (1c8d712b-b4ed-42ff-bb17-c2c8db2e166b$ROCeUoa1GXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

After you send the request with your actual values, the response body will include the OAuth token:

 

4. Generate the Certificate via Destination Service REST API

From the collection, open the request 'DestinationServiceCertificateGenerator'

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
Name (in payload of the body)Desired name of the created certificate (TestCert)
CN_value (in payload of the body)Common Name, represents the primary, specific domain name (MyDomain)
Base64_Encoded_CSR (in payload of the body)Paste the Base64 encoded value of .CSR that is created as a last step in the chapter '2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)'
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

In the response of the API call the HTTP 201 - Created should be appear as response code:

 

5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate

From the collection, open the request 'FetchNewCertificate' and a last step retrieve the created certificate chain.

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
name (in the URL section)In the previous step created name of the certificate (TestCert)
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

The API should return HTTP 200 OK, and the payload contains the newly created certificate chain, this can be found in the 'Content' field:

Save this value in a text file (base64.txt), because it is needed to Base64-decode the value to get the certificate chain. To perform the Base64 decoding open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing the recently saved file and run the command:

$base64 = Get-Content base64.txt -Raw
[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("certChain.txt", [Convert]::FromBase64String($base64))

This command will:

  • read the Base64 string from base64.txt
  • Convert it to bytes
  • Write the bytes to certChain.txt in the folder where the PowerShell was opened

Once the command completes, verify that the created file contains content like:

You can then save it as a .PEM file and generate a .PFX for client certificate based authentication, by referring to the blog “Client Certificate Authentication in SAP Cloud Integration: Configuration and Best Practices,” section 1.1/A, “Generating the Client Certificate (.PFX file) – Using OpenSSL.”

ℹ️BTP Connectivity Service on CN data centers signs the keypair using [CDC Cloud Root CA] ℹ️

Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)

This section explains how to create and load a client certificate in S/4HANA using transaction STRUST. If you’re connecting from S/4HANA, you can generate the CSR directly in STRUST - no OpenSSL needed. After generating the CSR, use the Destination Service API to sign it and obtain the client certificate, then import the signed certificate back into the client PSE in STRUST for client certificate–based authentication.

 

  1. Create an SSL client PSE in S/4HANA; for exact steps refer to 2. Configuration Steps in the Backend 

  2. Right‑click the PSE and choose Create. (Make sure you are in Change mode.)

  3. Generate new Client Certificate and sign the certificate: STRUST -> Edit -> Create certificate request.

  4. In the popup, select Use PSE algorithm as the Signature algorithm, and press Continue.

  5. Save the signing request, which should have the following format:
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----
    <CSR>
    -----END CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----

  6. Encode the CSR in Base64 (the Destination Service API expects a Base64 string without the PEM headers/footers).

  7. In the DestinationServiceCertificateGeneration request, copy the encoded CSR in the body in the parameter "CSR".

  8. After the certificate is signed by the Destination Service API, Base64‑decode it, then in STRUST choose Edit -> Import certificate response. Import the full certificate chain (from the decoded file).

Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP

Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:

In Neo, inbound client-certificate authentication is configured by uploading the caller’s public certificate and mapping it to a platform user. This is done in the Certificate-to-User Mapping monitor in the Operations view.

  1. Open your Cloud Integration tenant and switch to Operations.
  2. Go to Manage Security > Certificate-to-User Mapping.
  3. Click Add and upload the client’s public certificate file (.cer). Upload only the leaf certificate of the client, not the chain.
  4. Assign a user name to this certificate. Use the technical platform user that should run the iFlow.
  5. Save the mapping.

Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:

To make the client certificate available to your Integration flows, create an external-certificate service key:

  1. Go to the SAP BTP cockpit, open your subaccount, and navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Select your desired 'Process Integration Runtime' with plan 'integration-flow' service instance
  3. Choose Create under Service Keys
  4. Enter a name for the service key
  5. Set Key Type to External Certificate and paste the PEM-encoded client certificate (only the first/leaf certificate, not the chain) into the External Certificate field
  6. Select Create

For more detailed steps, refer to the KBA 3297437  - Client Cert Authentication for Inbound calls to CPI tenants on CF.

Conclusion

The upcoming EKU deprecation underscores the need to proactively adjust certificate management practices in SAP Integration Suite landscapes. By implementing the recommended steps, organizations can maintain secure, compliant client certificate authentication for inbound scenarios. Preparing early helps prevent disruptions and ensures integrations continue to run smoothly as industry standards evolve.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Purnima and I'm Passionate About Building My Career in SAP
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

I’m Purnima, a B.Tech graduate from Chalapathi Institute of Technology, currently working on an SAP FICO implementation project.

 

Through this experience, I am developing strong knowledge in Financial Accounting (FI) and Controlling (CO), working with areas such as General Ledger, Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, and Cost Center Accounting. I am actively involved in configuration, testing, and business process analysis.

I enjoy understanding how SAP supports financial operations, enhances cost control, and enables data-driven decision-making. I am excited to further build my expertise in SAP FICO.

 

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become a skilled SAP FICO consultant who can help organizations manage their financial processes efficiently. I aim to contribute to business growth by providing accurate financial insights and improving decision-making through SAP solutions.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In the next 5 years, I see myself as an experienced SAP FICO consultant with strong expertise in implementation and support projects. I aim to work on real-time business scenarios, handle complex requirements, and continuously upgrade my skills in SAP and related technologies.

What is your hobby?

My hobbies include learning new technologies, improving my communication skills, and exploring SAP-related concepts. I also enjoy spending time enhancing my knowledge, which helps me grow both personally and professionally.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The best lesson my parents taught me is the importance of hard work, discipline, and consistency. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals, be patient during challenges, and never give up. This mindset helps me stay motivated and continuously improve in my career.

Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

  1. To become a successful SAP FICO consultant with strong expertise in financial processes
  2. To continuously learn and upgrade my skills in SAP and new technologies
  3. To achieve financial independence and stability
  4. To contribute to organizational success through my knowledge and work
  5. To maintain a good balance between my personal and professional life.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

This year, I had the incredible opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2026, and it was truly a rewarding experience. The event brought together developers, architects, and technology enthusiasts from across the ecosystem, creating a vibrant space for learning and collaboration.


From insightful keynote sessions to hands-on workshops, every moment was packed with value. I particularly enjoyed exploring the latest innovations in AI, cloud technologies, and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). The live demos and real-world use cases helped bridge the gap between theory and practical application.

One of the most valuable aspects was networking—connecting with like-minded professionals, SAP experts, and industry leaders. These conversations opened new perspectives and ideas that I’m excited to take forward in my work.

 

And now let’s hear from you!

I would love to hear your thoughts.

What is your personal life dream?

Feel free to share your answer in the comments and tell us about your goals and dreams.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Kavuri Revathi and I'm excited to begin my journey in the SAP world.
entry.content:
My name is Kavuri Revathi and I'm student at Chalapathi Institute of Engineering And Technology in Guntur, Andhra Pradesh, India.

I am currently pursuing my B.Tech final year in Computer Science and Engineering. Along with my academic studies, I have completed my certification in SAP End-to-End Business Process. This helped me understand how different business functions like procurement, inventory management, and finance are integrated within SAP systems.

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative:

What is your professional life dream?

My career goal is to become a skilled SAP consultant who can help organizations improve their business processes and efficiency. I want to work on real-time projects and contribute to digital transformation by using SAP solutions.

What is your Hobby?


My hobbies include travelling, learning new technologies and improving my technical skills . I enjoy exploring SAP concepts, practicing different business scenarios, and enhancing my problem-solving abilities. I also like reading about emerging technologies and how they impact industries.

What is the best lesson you have learned so far?

One of the most important lessons I have learned is the value of continuous learning and consistency. In the field of technology, staying updated and practicing regularly is key to success. This mindset helps me stay motivated and focused on my goals.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore experience

Recently, I had the opportunity to attend the SAP TechEd event in Bangalore, and it was truly an incredible experience for me. It was inspiring to meet SAP experts and senior professionals and see how humble and approachable they were while interacting with participants.

I also got the chance to connect with people from different organizations, which helped me understand the wide range of opportunities available in the SAP ecosystem. Through these interactions, I learned how companies are adopting new technologies like Artificial Intelligence and Cloud to transform their business processes.

During the event, I also appeared for the Generative AI certification exam at the center. Even though there were a few technical issues, the support team handled the situation very calmly and professionally, which made the experience smooth and comfortable.

The sessions and speaker presentations were highly insightful. Overall, this experience motivated me to continue learning and building my career in SAP and emerging technologies.

And now let’s hear from you!

I would love to hear your thoughts.

What is the best lesson you have learned?

Feel free to share your answer in the comments and tell us about your goals and dreams.

entry.title: CAPEX BUDGET PR/PO LEVEL PUBLIC CLOUD
entry.content:
CAPEX Budgeting,

In SAP Public Cloud, I want to set a budget at asset class-wise, read all blogs and posts, but not get an exact answer or process flow.
My client is using PR to initiate CAPEX. When I use Account assignment A, I have no option to give WBS Element; without that, I know no budget will be consumed. If I use assignment P, then SAP asks for WBS and GL account, and that GL account then appears DR. Given GL A/C and CR GRIR account, that is not required 

If I manually give AUC Asset GL, then SAP does not consume the budget i believe this is due to the nature of GL to work with WBS; we need a cost element, not B/S GL
how i set budget on PR/PO level for CAPEX

entry.title: A step-by-step end-to-end integration guide for integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM External API Management enables integration with external task management systems (Jira & Azure DevOps), ITSM systems (like ServiceNow), and communication tools & chat systems (Microsoft Teams) via webhooks and API subscriptions.

Recently, we had a requirement in a customer implementation project to notify the project team members via Microsoft Teams and channel workflow tasks whenever new project tasks, user stories, roadmap tasks, requirements and defects are created.

Here is the step-by-step end-to-end integration guide for integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams.  

  1. MS Teams Activities:

Create a Team “Basis Team” with Channel Name “Cloud ALM” in MS Teams

 

Create incoming webhooks with Microsoft Teams workflows.

 

Copy the webhook link (below, scrambled for restricting external usage)

https://defaultd93f1efe384dYYYabb9605734XXXda.ee.environment.api.powerplatform.com:443/powerautomate/automations/direct/workflows/8d869ca29b7e4b0cae87cb4ffdfac196/triggers/manual/paths/invoke?api-version=1&sp=%2Ftriggers%2Fmanual%2Frun&sv=1.0&sig=kdr4Ge5uttapaBCiGWZYcxlMizZ-d-B-3p1jcYUPAwJQ

 

  1. Cloud ALM subaccount Activities:

You need access to the SAP Cloud ALM subaccount in SAP BTP Cockpit.

Your user requires the “Connectivity and Destination Administrator” role in the SAP Cloud ALM subaccount.

Create a destination in SAP BTP Cockpit.

 

 

 

 

  1. Cloud ALM application Activities:

Click on External API Management

Webhook Management

Add a new webhook

 

Mappings Management – use the built-in for “Task to MS Teams Chat” listed below while adding a subscription

Subscription Management

Add new Subscription

 

  1. Assign the subscription created above to the Cloud ALM projects

In Manage Projects à Assign “CLOUDALM_MSTEAMS_SUBSCRIPTION” under the subscriptions tab for the required Item Type, say “Project Task”, “Roadmap Task”, “User Story”, “Requirement”, “Defect”.

 

  1. Test the workflow

Now, it’s time to create a project task and check the MS team's channel for notifications.

 

 

Once the project task is created, it should show up in the console section of External API Management (as Task created with 200 OK delivery status)

Finally, the workflows task will appear in Teams and Channels for your action.

Conclusion:

Integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams is a brilliant move for project transparency. It transforms your ALM environment from a static repository into a living, breathing workspace where the team can react to newly created project tasks, user stories, defects or requirements in real-time.

The above setup applies even to SAP Cloud ALM operations monitoring (say, Health Monitoring, Jobs Monitoring, Integration Monitoring, etc.) to send alert notifications to MS Teams chat using the mapping “Event situation to MS Teams chat” and the resource type in the subscription management must be set to “Event Situation”.

entry.title: SAP Community Hobbies Series : [Not Just a Hobby: A Story of Small Classrooms and Big Dreams]
entry.content:
Hi, My name is Abhijna,  I am  an engineering student and I would like to share about my journey of teaching and connecting with young minds as part of the SAP Community Hobbies series.  I am a student with a deep curiosity for connections in all forms. Whether it’s understanding how data travels through networks or building meaningful connections with people, I find myself drawn to both worlds equally. With a strong interest in electronics and networking, I enjoy exploring how systems work, and at the same time, how small human interactions can create lasting impact. And somewhere between these two, I found a journey that’s just getting started.

Coming from a family where teaching has always been more than just a profession, I grew up witnessing the quiet impact it can create. Among all those influences, my grandfather has been my biggest inspiration. Not because of titles or achievements, but because of the way people still remember him, with respect, gratitude, and stories of how he shaped their lives..

About a year or two ago, I visited a  school, one where my grandfather had once taught. I didn’t go there with a plan. It was just a visit. But what I experienced there stayed with me. The respect people had for him, the way they remembered him, and the number of lives he had impacted, it made me pause. It made me think. What if I could become even a small reason for someone to move forward in life?

That thought stayed. And eventually, it turned into action. I went back not as a visitor this time, but as someone who wanted to give. I started small. I spent a week teaching dance, just to connect. And then I began talking to students about things they usually don’t get exposed to, what engineering is, what opportunities exist, what lies beyond what they see every day.

And that’s when I truly understood something. There is a different kind of energy in  school classrooms. The students there don’t just sit and listen , they want to learn. There is curiosity, there is respect, and there is this quiet hunger for knowledge. They are waiting for someone to show them what’s possible.

And being able to stand there, even for a short time, felt like a responsibility , and also a privilege. Of course, it wasn’t always easy.

Standing in front of students who are just a few years younger than you makes you question yourself. Will they understand? Will this make sense to them? Especially when you’re trying to explain concepts like electronics or embedded systems, things that even we find complex. But over time, I realized something simple. If you can relate knowledge to life, anyone can understand it. When I started breaking things down into examples they could see and feel , like explaining sensors through real-life situations , their response changed completely. It wasn’t confusion anymore. It was curiosity. One moment, in particular, stays with me. At the end of a session, a 7th-grade student came up to me and confidently named two sensors we had discussed. Just two. But that moment meant everything. Because it showed me that something had stayed with him. And sometimes, that’s all it takes.

A small understanding. A small spark. A small beginning.

There are many misconceptions around  schools, that it’s difficult to engage, that students may not be interested, or that opportunities to contribute are limited. But my experience has been the opposite. When approached in the right way, schools are open. And the students? They are ready. All they need is someone to show up.

When I think about how this connects to the SAP world, I see a strong parallel. SAP is all about creating impact through structured systems, data, and processes. Similarly, what I do involves identifying gaps, organizing knowledge, and delivering it in a way that creates meaningful outcomes. It’s about enabling growth, improving accessibility, and making information useful ,just like SAP does, but in a human, grassroots way.

This journey has also changed the way I look at learning. To teach even a little, I’ve realized I need to keep learning constantly. It’s not a one-way process. It’s a cycle , where giving and learning go hand in hand. And somewhere along the way, this stopped feeling like just a hobby. It became something more personal. Something that connects who I am, where I come from, and what I want to become. Because at the end of the day, it’s not always about doing something big. Sometimes, it’s just about showing up in a small classroom , and becoming a reason for someone to dream bigger.

entry.title: Troubleshooting Guide: "To Be Hired" Status Not Updating in SAP SuccessFactors Manage Positions
entry.content:
Troubleshooting by Scenario:

1. Issues During Import Processes

Scenario A: Job Information Import Not Updating "To Be Hired" Status

When this occurs:

  • Assign or unassign users via job information import

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Follow-up processes not configured

  • Symptoms: Import completes successfully but "To Be Hired" status remains unchanged
  • Solution: Configure HrisSLEventPublishConfig in Manage Data
    1. Navigate to Manage Data
    2. Create "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" object
    3. If object not found: Configure Object Definitions → Search for "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" → Make Corrections → Add label → Save
    4. Grant user permissions under Miscellaneous Permissions

Cause 2: Position Management Settings not enabled

  • Symptoms: Imports work but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t adapt
  • Solution: Enable adaptation setting
    1. Go to Position Management Settings → Import tab
    2. Set "Adapt Position 'To Be Hired' Status After Imports" to YES

Scenario B: Termination Import Not Updating Status

When this occurs:

  • Importing termination records
  • API-based termination processing

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Termination imports don’t trigger "To Be Hired" adaptation
  • Reason: System only adapts when position changes occur, not employment status changes
  • Workaround: Manual position record update required after termination

2. Issues with UI Operations

Scenario C: MSS UI Changes Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using "Change Job and Compensation Info" in MSS
  • Making position changes through Edit button

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Position previously assigned via Job Information History

  • Symptoms: MSS UI changes don’t trigger "To Be Hired" status updates
  • Solution: This is expected behavior - position won’t adapt if originally assigned through Job History
  • Workaround: Use Add New Employee or direct MSS UI for initial assignments

Cause 2: Missing FTE calculation rules

  • Symptoms: Position assignment completes but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t change
  • Solution: Configure FTE calculation business rules
    1. Create business rule using "Calculate Full-Time Equivalent (FTE)" scenario
    2. Set as onChange rule (on custom standard-hours field) OR Set as onSave rule (on jobInfo element)

Cause 3: Missing mandatory fields in position

  • Symptoms: Position change is successful but "To Be Hired" not updated
  • Solution: Complete required position fields
    1. Navigate to Manage Positions
    2. Find affected position → Make Correction
    3. Fill all mandatory fields

Scenario 😧 Job Information History Changes

When this occurs:

  • Making corrections in Job Information History portlet
  • Adding new records in job history

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Changes in Job Information History don’t update "To Be Hired"
  • Solution: Use proper channels for position updates:
    1. Add New Employee wizard for new hires
    2. MSS UI "Change Job and Compensation Info" or Edit button for existing employees

3. Issues with Onboarding and Recruiting Integration

Scenario E: API-Based Onboarding Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using APIs for onboarding processes

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Position Management doesn’t support follow-up processes for Onboarding API changes
  • Manual Solution: Update position records manually after API-based hiring

Scenario F: Crossboarding/Internal Hire Issues

When this occurs:

  • Initiating crossboarding via Recruiting
  • Internal transfers and promotions

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Cause: "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" setting disabled
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Admin Center → Onboarding General Settings
    2. Enable "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" option

4. Issues with Multiple Incumbents and FTE Management

Scenario G: Multiple Incumbent Positions

When this occurs:

  • Positions allowing multiple incumbents
  • FTE-based position management

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: positionControlled field not editable

  • Symptoms: FTE calculations incorrect despite correct assignments
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Configure Object Definitions → Position
    2. Set positionControlled field to "editable"

Cause 2: FTE setting configuration

  • Symptoms: Position not updating when FTE appears reached
  • Solution: Adjust Position Management Settings
    1. Navigate to Position Management Settings → General
    2. Change "Set or Reset 'To Be Hired' Status if an Incumbent’s 'FTE' is Changed" to "Always" (instead of "Only if Planned FTE Value is Reached")

Cause 3: Effective dating logic with future hires

  • Symptoms: Current FTE reached but status not updating due to future-dated assignments
  • Solution: Ensure employees assigned with appropriate effective dates that reflect current staffing needs

5. Issues with Position Reclassification

Scenario H: Position Reclassification Creating New Positions

When this occurs:

  • Using Event Reasons with "Position Reclassification" follow-up activity
  • Position updates result in new position creation

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Search setting disabled

  • Solution:
    1. Go to Position Management Settings
    2. Set "Search for Position in Position Reclassification" to "Yes"

Cause 2: Field matching issues

  • Symptoms: System cannot find matching vacant position
  • Solution: Ensure vacant positions have same field values (Department, Division, Custom Fields) or include them in Job-to-Position sync rules

6. Issues with Future Dating and Transition Periods

Scenario I: Future-Dated Hires with transition period

When this occurs:

  • Processing candidates with future start dates
  • Managing transition periods between incumbents

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: "To Be Hired" reflects effective date, not process completion date.  It is currently not possible to update the 'to be hired' status to NO, if a person is terminated in the future and the 'successor' gets assigned to the same position BEFORE the other incumbent is leaving.

To retrigger the "To Be Hired" status adaptation, navigate to Admin Alerts → Position Follow-up Processes Alerts and retrigger the process

If it still fails, you can raise a ticket to product support.

Reference:
Enabling To Be Hired Status Adaptation on the Position (Change from Employee Data Import) | SAP Help Portal
Adapt the To Be Hired Status on the Position (Change from UI) | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Extend customer records with credit management data
entry.content:
1. Use the migration object “Customer - extend existing record by credit management data” to create credit management data for existing customers.
  2. Create the required credit management profile data using this migration object.
  3. Create the required credit management segment data using this migration object (for example, segment assignments).
  4. Note: Updating or overwriting existing fields or records is not possible with this migration object; it can only add new credit management data to extend existing customer records.
  5. refer to: Customer - extend existing record by credit management data (Help Portal)
  6. refer to: Community discussion: mass update credit limit

entry.title: Business AI Makes the Data Layer More Important, Not Less
entry.content:
Many organizations are approaching enterprise AI in the same way. They invest heavily in the AI layer: the agents, the models, the application experience. The data layer gets rationalized as infrastructure, a necessary but unglamorous foundation that the AI sits on top of. The implicit assumption is that once the AI capability is in place, the data layer becomes more of a commodity. The agents are the product now. The data is just the pipe.

SAP is making a different bet.

The architecture behind SAP Business AI is built on the premise that the AI layer and the data layer are not in competition for strategic importance. Quite the opposite. SAP Business AI runs on top of SAP Business Data Cloud, a purpose-built foundation that combines harmonized business data, a Knowledge Graph, and a Customer Data Platform into a contextually rich, semantically governed data foundation. The design choice is explicit: the richer the data foundation, the more capable and trustworthy the AI on top of it.

What the Economists Got Right

In Prediction Machines (2018), economists Agrawal, Gans, and Goldfarb made a precise claim: AI is a dramatic drop in the cost of prediction. When something gets dramatically cheaper, classical economics tells you what happens next. The value shifts to the complements. The prediction becomes the commodity; what becomes scarce, and therefore valuable, is what makes the prediction accurate: clean, structured, domain-specific data, and the judgment about what to do with the output.

In Power and Prediction (2022), the same authors extended the argument from prediction to decision-making. Cheap prediction changes who holds power in any decision chain. The question shifts from "can we predict this?" to "who defines the problem the AI reasons about, who curates the inputs, and who is accountable for the output?" The agents handling those decisions are not reasoning in a vacuum. They are reasoning over data, and the quality of the reasoning is bounded, at every step, by the quality of the context it receives.

The 2018 argument was a forecast. Today it is a description. Base models are converging. The output quality of leading AI systems, given the same inputs, is not as different as the marketing would suggest. What is not converging is the quality of the inputs: the richness of the context, the precision of the semantics, the trustworthiness of the lineage. That is where the value is accumulating.

This leads directly to the key insight for enterprise AI. When you deploy an agent on top of your data platform, you are not outsourcing the data problem. You are raising the stakes for it. Every error in the data layer propagates forward into every agent action. Every ambiguity in the semantics becomes an ambiguity in the output. The richer and more reliable the context, the more capable the agent. The thinner the context, the more confidently wrong the agent becomes.

The Inversion Nobody Announces

Here is what this means for SAP's architecture, stated plainly.

When Business AI arrives as an application layer on top of BDC, the temptation is to treat BDC as foundational infrastructure: necessary, stable, not the interesting part. The interesting part is what the agent does. The demos show the agent. The keynotes talk about the agent.

But the agent is not the competitive differentiator. Two enterprises using the same Business AI models, the same agents, and the same application layer will still get different results. Different accuracy. Different relevance. Different reliability. The difference is not in the model. It is in what the model reasons over.

The introduction of Business AI does not diminish the importance of the data layer. It amplifies it. Every capability the agent gains is a capability that can be better or worse depending on the quality of the data foundation underneath it.

This is not a warning. It is an opportunity. SAP's architecture, specifically BDC, the Knowledge Graph, and CDP working together, is designed to provide exactly what reasoning agents require: contextual, semantically rich, governed data that makes AI outputs trustworthy rather than merely statistically plausible.

What BDC Actually Provides

SAP Business Data Cloud does something specific that matters for this argument. It does not simply store business data. It exposes that data as semantically rich, AI-ready data products, with documented logic, clear lineage, and governance built into the product definition from the start.

The distinction is significant. A flat database has rows and columns. A governed data product has meaning. It carries the business semantics that allow an agent to reason accurately rather than pattern-match on surface features. When an AI agent consumes a data product from BDC, it is not just receiving numbers. It is receiving numbers in context: what they measure, where they come from, how fresh they are, what they mean in the business domain.

For AI reasoning, that difference is the difference between an output you can act on and an output that sounds correct but cannot be trusted. Hundreds of customers have already signed on to BDC in less than a year, a pace that reflects confidence in the platform's direction. The hypergrowth is not about the features. It is about the recognition that AI without governed context is a very expensive way to hallucinate at enterprise scale.

SAP BDC and HANA Extend What Data Products Alone Cannot Deliver

Governed data products are the foundation. But the Knowledge Graph, powered by SAP HANA, and the Customer Data Platform extend what the AI can do with that foundation in ways that data products alone cannot.

The Knowledge Graph is often described in retrieval terms: it helps agents find the right information faster. That is accurate but incomplete. The more important function is relational. A Knowledge Graph makes the connections between entities explicit and computable. Not just "this is a customer" and "this is an order" as separate records. But "this customer, operating in this region, under these contract terms, has this service history, and is currently associated with these risk signals." For agents that reason, relationships matter more than records. A reasoning system given isolated data points produces isolated insights. A reasoning system given explicit relationships can surface conclusions that the data, taken row by row, would never reveal. The Knowledge Graph is the difference between a data foundation the agent can browse and one it can actually think with.

BDC also includes a Customer Data Platform, which adds what is often missing from purely operational data: the unified view of the person or organization on the receiving end of the decision. Business decisions, even deeply automated ones, ultimately concern customers, partners, and employees. An agent recommending a pricing action, flagging a service risk, or personalizing an engagement is reasoning about a specific relationship. CDP makes that relationship visible: not just transaction history, but the full behavioral and preference context that determines whether the action the agent proposes is appropriate for this customer in this moment. In the framework of Power and Prediction, this is the layer that moves AI outputs from statistically likely to actually relevant. The same recommendation, applied to two customers with identical transaction patterns but different relationship histories, may be exactly right for one and exactly wrong for the other. CDP is what allows the agent to tell the difference. It is where technology meets people, and in a world of autonomous agents making decisions at scale, it is where responsibility ultimately lives.

The Moat Is Not the Model

There is a version of enterprise AI strategy that treats the AI layer as the competitive moat. Deploy the most capable agents, get the best model, and differentiate through the technology. This strategy is becoming harder to defend by the quarter as model quality converges and agent frameworks commoditize.

The durable moat is the data foundation. The organization that has invested in a semantically coherent, governed, contextually rich data layer is in a fundamentally different position from one that treated data as a byproduct of operations. The former can deploy agents that reason reliably over enterprise reality. The latter deploys agents that reason confidently over whatever they happen to receive.

Agrawal, Gans, and Goldfarb wrote the economic argument for this in 2018 and extended it in 2022. They were describing a force that would reshape where value accumulates in technology, and they were right. SAP's architecture for BDC, the Knowledge Graph, and CDP reflects exactly this logic. The Business AI layer does not change the equation. It confirms it.

An agent reasoning over commodity data reaches the same wrong answer as your competitor's agent, just faster.

The prediction is the commodity. The context is the product.

entry.title: SAP User Experience Q1/2026 Update – Part 9: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile
entry.content:
This ninth and last post in my series gives an overview innovations in our UI tools and technologies, making it ever easier to design, build and integrate UIs, for both web and mobile applications. For designing apps, web and mobile, we have added AI content and more to the SAP Design System portal and UI Kits in Figma. For web developers, we have introduced the SAP Fiori development portal, as well as more AI support via MCP servers for SAPUI5 and for SAP Fiori elements, and Joule integration. Beyond that: have a look at how you can use AI to migrate to TypeScript, learn about the new Geomap control, and benefit from the new SAPUI5 runtime repository. Finally, discover the latest updates for mobile design and development with our SDKs and the mobile development kit.

Joule can also be seen as a technology for delivering a great user experience. To find out about recent innovations there, as well as for central entry points and services, have a look at this post, which also contains links to all the other parts of the series:

  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

In case you missed them, do have a look at my previous posts covering design and technology news, since what I mention here builds partly on what came before:

  • SAP User Experience Q1/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile. This post has a lot of information about extensibility, especially for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Do also have a look at the new SAP Extensibility Explorer.
  • SAP UX Q3/2025 Update – Part 7: UI Design and Technology for Web and Mobile.

First, we’ll have a look at news for creating web UIs, before looking at what’s new for mobile design and development.

 

News for Creating Web UIs

I’ve compiled the news into these sections:

  • SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements.
  • SAP Fiori Development Portal.
  • Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule.
  • SAP Fiori Tools News.

SAP Design System Portal and UI Kits – now with AI UI Elements

We recently introduced the new SAP Design System portal that brings together the product design guidelines SAP Fiori for web, SAP Fiori for iOS, and SAP Fiori for Android, as well as to the new digital design system guidelines. With its enhanced usability, simplified versioning and improved performance, designers (and developers!) get a better user experience compared with the previous SAP Fiori design guidelines.

End of last year we introduced these AI UI elements for web UIs, as you can see in Figure 1 below:

  • AI Acknowledgement.
  • AI Button.
  • AI Writing Assistant.

Figure 1: AI UI elements available in SAP Design System portal. Alt Text: Screenshot of the portal UI Elements page with a filter at the top containing “AI”, below that “3 results” showing three cards in a horizontal row for “AI Acknowledgement”, “AI Button” and “AI Writing Assistant”.

Get a complete overview of what’s new in the design system portal here

  • What’s New in the SAP Design System Portal?

UI Kits v8.0 and v9.0 – now with AI components

The new UI Kits available for designers in Figma make it easy for designers to design accessible UIs at enterprise scale, for web applications, mobile iOS and Android applications as well as wearables. The UI Kits provide reusable components for web and mobile, along with web floorplans, which give you what you need out-of-the-box.

In the meantime, we have released SAP Web UI Kit v8.0 which includes a full set of AI components: AI Button, AI Menu Button, AI Split Menu Button, AI Input, and AI Text Area.

The latest release, SAP Web UI Kit v9.0, continues to evolve the system with focused improvements that enhance visual quality, usability, and overall consistency across the kit. It brings:

  • Updated Icons:
    All icons have been refreshed with a 1.5px stroke (down from 1.6px). This improves anti-aliasing and results in cleaner, sharper visuals across interfaces.
  • Busy Indicator Enhancements:
    You now have two Busy Indicator options, static and animated, available on the same page. The new animated version is ready to use out of the box, saving time and implementation effort.
  • Redesigned Color Palette. Note that the previous color palette component has been removed in order to avoid confusion, and keep the kit cleaner and easier to navigate.

Find out more here:

  • New SAP Design System Portal for SAP Fiori Design Guidelines, and New UI Kits for Designers.

SAP Fiori Development Portal

I’m also happy to inform you about the new SAP Fiori development portal. It is designed to help developers maximize the benefits of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4, which is more than just a UI framework: it’s a metadata-driven approach that leverages the SAP Design System as its guiding principle, incorporating the most relevant design system options for typical business tasks.

Figure 2: The SAP Fiori development portal. Alt text: A screenshot showing the Introduction page. On the left a menu has entries Using the Portal, Building Blocks, Global Patterns, Floorplans, SAP Fiori Tools. The Introduction page has a full width card “Building Blocks” at the top, with a description and tiles representing various building blocks. Below that “Choose Your Option” heading above two side-by-side cards for “Floorplans” and “Customer Layout”, each with an image and description, and a “Learn More” button.

The SAP Fiori development portal puts building blocks front and center. These building blocks - such as tables, forms, filter bars, and charts - are orchestrated by SAP Fiori elements and form the basis of every OData V4 application. They provide standardized layouts and interaction patterns, ensuring UI consistency and boosting developer productivity. The great advantage of using building blocks is that you can use them to create a customer layout and yet still benefit from the advantages of the metadata driven approach: low code, built in enterprise-grade capabilities such as multi-language support, accessibility, security and performance, and design system consistency.

Find out more in this six-part series of blog posts, here is the first one, which contains links to the others:

  • Introducing the SAP Fiori Development Portal: Your Gateway to Rapid SAP Fiori App Creation (1 of 6).

We have also restructured our documentation for SAP Fiori elements, with separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, and better supporting the building block approach. Read more here:

  • SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure.

Using AI to Develop UIs – MCP Servers, Joule

The above posts from Q1/2025 and Q3/2025 introduced a number of powerful ways to accelerate UI development by using AI, such as:

  • Creating SAP Fiori elements apps from a sketch (in the Q1 post), now integrated in SAP Build Code and Joule (Q3 post)
  • Joule for SAPUI5 freestyle development (Q1 post)
  • AI-assisted auto-fix of coding errors with UI5 linter (Q3 post)

Recently, we have gone even further and introduced MCP servers for freestyle SAPUI5 development as well as for developing with SAP Fiori elements and the associated building blocks. These open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide AI agents with comprehensive UI5 and SAP Fiori elements knowledge, including best practices and project-aware context information, and thus provide developers with valuable support. Figure 3 shows examples.

Figure 3: Examples of the UI5 MCP Server (on the left) and the MCP Server for SAP Fiori (on the right) in action. Alt Text: both images show screenshots of a CLINE console view with a task entered as text at the top, followed by a list of steps which the system proposes to do to solve the task.

Read about these new MCP servers in these blog posts:

  • UI5 MCP Server.
  • MCP Server for SAP Fiori.
  • Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits

Alternatively, you can listen to these podcasts involving Tim Back and Florian Vogt, talking about the intricacies of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) within the SAP ecosystem:

  • Spotify: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.
  • Apple Podcast: UI5 & MCP - Navigating the Model Context Protocol in SAP.

Latest UI5 MCP Server Enhancements

The UI5 MCP server got extended to help migrate existing JavaScript code to TypeScript, as well as for generating UI Integration Cards.

New TypeScript Migration Feature

With the newest feature of the UI5 MCP server, UI5-specific TypeScript conversion assistance is now available to AI coding agents, making the task much easier. Find out more in this blog post:

  • UI5 MCP Server's New TypeScript Migration Feature - Real-World Experience and Best Practices

Generating UI Integration Cards

The UI5 MCP Server now supports the development of UI Integration Cards. In its latest version – v0.2.1 – it includes three new tools dedicated to UI Integration Cards:

  1. Best practice guidance for your agent.
  2. Scaffolding a new Integration Card.
  3. Validating the Card’s manifest.

These tools assist Large Language Models (LLMs) in creating new cards from scratch or editing existing ones, following the best practices for card development. Find out more here:

  • UI5 MCP Server Got Extended for UI Integration Cards Generation.

AI News for SAP Fiori Tools and MCP Server for SAP Fiori

The Project Accelerator can now be launched directly from the SAP Fiori generator, streamlining the initial project creation flow. The previously required staging step has been removed, allowing new projects to be created directly in the selected workspace folder. For projects created using the Project Accelerator, the Application Modeler now offers advanced editing capabilities, such as creating new fields and properties end-to-end, updating the data model, exposing the service, and consuming the changes in the UI in one coherent flow.

AI support has also been extended to mock data generation. Developers can generate realistic mock data using AI directly in the Application Modeler, and this capability is available for all applications whenever the Project Accelerator is enabled.

In parallel, the MCP server for SAP Fiori continues to expand. The server now exposes @sap-ux/create commands as CLI functionality, enabling reliable execution in MCP-supported workflows. App generation has been extended to support CAP Java projects, and a new “search_docs” tool allows the LLM (Large Language Model) to query SAP Fiori elements documentation, annotations, SAP Fiori tools documentation, and SAP Fiori Development Portal resources to quickly find relevant guidance and code snippets.

Joule Enhancement: Create Freestyle SAPUI5 Apps in SAP Build Code

You can now use Joule in SAP Build Code to create new freestyle SAPUI5 apps according to your requirements. The feature is not absolutely groundbreaking, as there are other tools doing a similar job like the Easy-UI5 templates and the recently published UI5 MCP Server. But:

  1. It brings the functionality right to the fingertips of developers using SAP Build Code – no other tools needed.
  2. It extends the template capabilities beyond what the Easy-UI5 templates provide.
  3. Implementing it within the Build Code Joule environment yielded interesting insights and further improvements.

Find out more about the feature, and in the second “making of” post, read about how Andreas Kunz went about building it, and what he learnt from doing it:

  • Joule in SAP Build Code can now create freestyle UI5 apps.
  • Inside Joule: Lessons from building an AI coding assistant for SAP Build.

SAP Fiori Tools News

In addition to the AI-enhancements mentioned above, we now provide expanded support for SAP Fiori elements building blocks, a new connection manager for SAP Systems in VS Code, and a feature to preview SAP Fiori web apps on mobile devices.

Expanded Support for SAP Fiori Elements Building Blocks

The following enhancements make it easier to adapt applications and allow developers to work more confidently and efficiently with SAP Fiori elements–based UIs.

Developers can now add more building block types to their apps with the Page Editor, including

  • rich text editors within custom sections,
  • custom columns for table building blocks, and
  • the page building block itself.

Managing custom pages is also more intuitive, as building blocks can now be deleted directly from the editor.

To support experimentation and iteration, undo and redo actions are now available when working on custom pages.

New Connection Manager for SAP Systems in VS Code

We have introduced the Connection Manager for SAP Systems as a dedicated Visual Studio Code extension for managing connections to remote SAP systems. The Connection Manager allows developers to create, store, test, and edit connection information that can be reused by the SAP Fiori generator as well as by preview features in SAP Fiori tools.

Preview SAP Fiori Web Apps on Mobile

When developing SAP Fiori applications, simulating a mobile experience in a desktop browser often isn’t enough to fully validate real-world behavior. Touch interactions, performance characteristics, and device-specific rendering can differ noticeably on physical phones and tablets.

To address this, SAP Fiori tools now supports previewing applications directly on real mobile devices. You do this by using @sap/ux-ui5-tooling version 1.19.1 or higher and creating a new run configuration and enabling Remote Access. When you start the run configuration, the terminal provides connection details, including a URL and a QR code, which can be opened directly on a phone or tablet to access the running app.

Find out more here:

  • SAP Fiori tools 2601 update – Mobile Device Preview, Building Blocks, and AI-Driven Productivity.
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter January 2026 (issue #38).
  • SAP Fiori Development newsletter issue #39 (March 2026).

 

News for Mobile Designers and Developers

Let’s first look at updates in the design guidelines and the SDKs, and then look at news for the mobile development kit (MDK).

Mobile Design Guideline and SDK Updates

We have introduced numerous new and enhanced patterns and components this last half-year. I have selected a small number to highlight here, further below you’ll find links to get the full overview.

New for AI

The following two new patterns have been introduced for both the SAP Fiori for Android and the SAP Fiori for iOS design guidelines.

AI Insights Pattern: We have introduced a new AI Insights Pattern, which describes how to present AI insights optimally on mobile devices, as shown in Figure 4. It provides a standardized way to display a summary, analysis, or suggestion generated with AI. This pattern can be applied to a full-screen or a card depending on the scope of content generated by AI.

AI Handoff Pattern: We have created a new guideline describing the AI Handoff Pattern, which defines how a user should transition between contextual embedded AI features and the Joule conversational panel.

Figure 4: Two new AI patterns: AI Insights Pattern and AI Handoff Pattern. Alt Text: On the left two image for AI Insights Pattern, first a full mobile phone screen with title and subtitle at the top, followed by two insights sections, each with two slots for content, at the bottom a user feedback row with thumbs up and thumbs down icons, above a an AI generate button. On the right a diagram for the AI Handoff Pattern showing a schematic mobile module with arrow to and from a schematic Joule UI.

Further news

I have picked two stand-out updates, also made available for Android and iOS.

New Illustrations: We have introduced the new illustrations, that were also introduced in the web, to the mobile design system and the mobile SDKs. You can see a few examples on the left in Figure 5 below.

More flexible card layouts: We offer far more flexibility now in how you use cards to summarize information. For example, you can create wider cards that have the image on the left or the right, and the content block next to it. With the new flex item component, the card’s main header can now be customized to hold more content by adding tags and status info labels outside, on top, between, or below the title and subtitle. Two examples are shown in Figure 5 on the right.

Figure 5: New illustrations and more flexible card layout and content options. Alt Text: On the left three different-size “My Daily Tasks” cards, with images for “You’r all caught up”, “Oops” and “Congratulations”. On the right two card examples: at the top a slim wide card with title, subtitle and one line text on the left, image on the right; below that a larger card with image on the left, title and details with colored indicators and buttons on the right.

Find out more here:

  • Introducing SAP Fiori for Android/iOS 25.8: Innovative Enhancements for Android and iOS.

For an overview of all updates, have a look at the what’s new sections in the SAP Design System portal and the SAP Mobile Services Documentation

  • SAP Fiori for Android design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for Android 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for Android: What’s New in Release 26.1.2 and earlier releases.
  • SAP Fiori for iOS design: What's New for SAP BTP SDK for iOS 26.1 and older.
  • SAP BTP SDK for iOS: What’s New in Version 26.1.2 and earlier releases.

Mobile Development Kit (MDK) News

The Mobile Development Kit, available as part of SAP Mobile Services, enables developers and technical business users to build cross-platform mobile applications. It allows you to build your application once, in an integrated development environment (SAP Business Application Studio / VSCode extension) and run it natively on Mobile Android and iOS devices.  

Here I have chosen to highlight one area: enhancements to the calander control, which now supports date range selection as well as allowing you to highlight special events or statuses directly on the calendar.

The new date range selection displays the calendar in a month-view format and allows multiple months to be shown. Users can scroll vertically through the calendar months. This is ideal if you need to plan events or manage schedules over extended periods, since users can select a time period with a start date and an end date (see Figure 6 below):

  • The first date selected becomes the start date of the range.
  • The second date selected becomes the end date of the range.

Figure 6: Date range selection supported by the mobile development kit provided by SAP Mobile Service. Alt Text: An image of an iPhone and an Android phone next to each other. The iPhone with the light Morning Horizon theme and a date selection Sep 16 – Sep 29, 2025, the other with the dark Evening Horizon theme and a date selection Mon Sep 29 to Fri Oct 17 2025.

Find out more here:

  • Blog post: What’s new in Mobile development kit client 25.9.
  • Documentation: What’s New in Mobile Development Kit 25.9.1 and earlier releases.

 

Further Information

For creating web applications, do register for the upcoming UI5con virtual conference.

Learn more about it at https://openui5.org/ui5con/faq.html, including how to attend, sponsor, or propose a topic for a session.

For links to design and development tools and technology, have a look at the SAP Fiori topic page in the SAP Community, or go directly to these pages:

  • SAP Design System.
  • SAP Fiori tools.
  • SAPUI5.
  • SAP Fiori elements.
  • SAP Mobile technologies.

 

Final words

As you can see from all seven posts in the series, we continue to progress rapidly with the user experience of our product portfolio, especially with regards to AI-assistance.

Part 1 of the series lists all the parts, and links to those parts that have already been published:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

For general information on design at SAP and SAP Fiori, check out:

  • www.sap.com/design.
  • www.sap.com/fiori.
  • SAP Community: SAP Fiori topic page.

entry.title: CAP Initializr: Generate SAP CAP Projects in Seconds
entry.content:
Building SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming) projects can be repetitive, time-consuming, and sometimes confusing—especially when setting up the right structure, dependencies, and configurations.

That’s exactly why we built CAP Initializr.

In this post, we’ll walk through:

  • What CAP Initializr is

  • How it works

  • Why it’s useful

  • Who can benefit from it

  • How you can try it out and give feedback

Try the MVP

Try CAP Initializr here:

https://react-frontend.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/

Give Us Feedback

We are currently in the MVP stage, and your feedback is extremely valuable.

Share your feedback here:
CAP Initializer Survey – Fill out form

What is CAP Initializer?

CAP Initializr is a project generation tool that helps developers instantly create ready-to-run SAP CAP projects for:

  • Node.js runtime

  • Java (Spring Boot) runtime

Instead of manually setting up folders, dependencies, and configurations, CAP Initializr does everything for you—automatically.

How It Works

The idea is simple:
You select your preferences → we generate your project → you download and run.

Step-by-step flow:

  1. Enter project details

    • Project name

    • Package name (for Java)

    • Runtime (Node.js / Java)

  2. Select features

    • Database (H2, SQLite, HANA, etc.)

    • App Router

    • Additional services (like XSUAA, MTA, etc.)

  3. Project generation happens automatically
    Behind the scenes:

    • Base templates are copied

    • Runtime-specific structure is applied

    • Feature templates are merged

    • Dependencies are injected

    • Configurations are adjusted

    • Final project is zipped

  4. Download and run

  • You get a ready-to-use CAP project instantly

What Makes It Powerful?

Smart Template System

Each feature (like DB, App Router) can bring:

  • Code templates

  • Config files

  • Dependencies

Everything is modular and reusable.

Automatic Dependency Handling

  • Node.js
    → Dependencies added to package.json

  • Java
    → Dependencies injected into srv/pom.xml

  • Default dependencies are added automatically when needed.

Runtime Flexibility

Supports both:

  • Node.js CAP

  • Java CAP (Spring Boot + CDS)

So teams can choose their preferred stack.

Advantages

Saves Time

No need to manually:

  • Create folders

  • Configure CDS

  • Add dependencies

  • Setup Maven / NPM

Reduces Errors

Avoid common issues like:

  • Missing dependencies

  • Incorrect configurations

  • Broken project structures

Standardization

Ensures all projects follow:

  • Same structure

  • Same best practices

  • Same configurations

Perfect for teams.

Extensible Design

Easily add:

  • New features

  • New templates

  • New runtimes

Who Should Use This?

CAP Developers

Quickly bootstrap new projects without repetitive setup.

Enterprise Teams

Standardize CAP project creation across teams.

Students / Beginners

Focus on learning CAP instead of struggling with setup.

Authors (Scholars)

  • Aryan Tandon

  • Saptarshi Basu

entry.title: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
entry.content:
Hello,
My name is Patrick Schad, and I’m a Product Manager for SAP Build. In this post, I’d like to share important updates about the evolution of SAP Build Apps, what these changes mean for you, and how we’ll support your transition to our unified platform.

 

What's happening?

As part of the continued evolution and simplification of the SAP Build portfolio, SAP Build Apps is being deprecated as a standalone product. Moving forward, customers can leverage SAP Build unified offering, that combines application development, process automation, and business site creation into a single platform that supports low-code, code-first, and generative AI capabilities. 

 

What does this mean to you?

We recognize that change is never easy, and decisions like this are made with careful consideration. We are here to help outline what it means for you and your organization and are committed to supporting you every step of the way throughout this transition.

As of 23rd of March 2026, SAP Build Apps will be retired. Please note that there is no immediate impact on existing customers. Whether you have BTPEA/PAYG/CPEA or subscription-based licensing, you will continue to have full access to SAP Build Apps for the duration of your current contract. We will honor all existing commitments, including SLAs and maintenance, and your service will remain fully supported and operational through the end of your agreement.

 

Why are we making this change and what is the path forward?

We’ve listened closely to your feedback. As part of the ongoing evolution and simplification of the SAP Build portfolio, we are focusing on a more purpose-built approach that aligns with where the market is heading and where we can best support your needs.

Going forward, customers can leverage SAP Build offering, which unifies application development, process automation, and business site creation into a single platform that supports low-code, code-first, and generative AI capabilities.

This transition aligns with SAP’s broader strategy to simplify the commercial model and deliver a more integrated development experience across both SAP and non-SAP environments. It also allows us to focus our engineering and product investments where they can drive the greatest value and impact for your organization.

 

The Transition to SAP Build

With the retirement of SAP Build Apps, SAP Build has emerged as the comprehensive tool for all your extension, automation and application development needs. Designed to bring together low-code and pro-code capabilities ranging from drag-and-drop functionalities to advanced, AI-based code generation. SAP Build allows both developers and business experts to build and extend applications and automate processes in ways that work best for their requirements, allowing for greater developer flexibility and customization, tailored to specific business requirements and needs driving efficiency like never before.

If you're currently using SAP Build Apps, now is the time to explore how SAP Build can empower your teams with its advanced capabilities. This transition ensures you have access to the latest features, and alignment with SAP’s innovation roadmap.

 

Technical Migration from SAP Build Apps Standalone Offering to SAP Build

SAP Build Apps Frontend Projects

A direct migration path for existing SAP Build Apps frontend projects into the unified SAP Build offering is not available.

As you plan your next steps, we recommend leveraging the pro-code capabilities within SAP Build to recreate and further evolve your current and future applications. Our teams are here to support you throughout this process and help identify the best approach for your specific use cases.

 

SAP Build Apps Backend Projects

To transfer existing data from SAP Build Apps backend projects into the unified SAP Build offering, we recommend a manual approach:

In your SAP Build Apps backend project, open each entity in the data browser and use the Export option to download your data as a .csv file. Repeat this step for each entity.

In your new CAP project within SAP Build, import the downloaded .csv files. (Prerequisite: ensure that a table with the relevant fields has already been created in your CAP backend.)

For detailed instructions on uploading data into your CAP backend, please refer to this guide.

Please find here some answers to the frequently asked questions:

QuestionAnswer

How long will SAP Build Apps be supported?

We support and maintain the product until the end of your contract date.

Which tool can I replace SAP Build Apps with and how can I realize my use case?

The successor SKU is the SAP Build offering that provides a comprehensive and easy way to create applications as well as automations, business sites, native mobile apps, custom SAP Joule skills and Agents.

What advantages does SAP Build offer?

SAP Build is a comprehensive tool for all your extension, automation, and application development needs. It combines low-code and pro-code capabilities, from intuitive drag-and-drop functionality to advanced AI-powered code generation.

Can I use my SAP Build Apps project from the standalone offering and import it into the SAP Build offering?

No, this is not supported. Please refer to the help portal documentation for applicable migration paths.

What happens with ongoing web application projects?

Ongoing projects should be transitioned to our SAP Build offering. If the project is close to go-live and the SAP Build Apps backend projects are in use, we highly recommend switching to CAP.

What happens with my ongoing native mobile app projects?

We recommend using the mobile development kit and native SDKs, part of SAP Build offering / SAP Mobile services.

Are there any further features and roadmap items planned for SAP Build Apps?

No, there will be no additional features or data center support for SAP Build Apps going forward.

Will pricing change with the transition?

Yes, depending on the use case. For more information, please refer to the pricing page.

 

Next Steps

  • Learn more about the latest SAP Build offering
  • Get a 30 days access for a free trial to explore all SAP Build capabilities
  • Discover the SAP Build pricing model on our pricing page.
  • Further information mentioned in the help portal.

If you have any questions, concerns, or would like to discuss what this means for your specific setup, please don’t hesitate to reach out to your account manager or leave a comment below. We’re here to support you every step of the way.

entry.title: Re: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
@MateuszPiotrowski 

"As I said, there is no way to retry individual message from EM queue."
I would treat this as considerable Cons definitely.  Do you agree?

There could be an addition where the iflow checks if the error occurred due to an error which can be corrected ( a mapping issue in CPI or an master data issue in the receiver side ) later in time.  If the error falls to this category then the iFlow saves this message into the datastore , creates a Cloud ALM or JIRA ticket, and once the user confirms the data is fixed, a reprocessing iFlow would reprocess the message from the datastore. 

There is a very good extension of the PipeLine concept where also the Data Store is used for saving the erroneous message for later use.

Using Cloud ALM or JIRA for individual message reprocessing could be a very good topic to be discussed. 
Because it seems that Event Mesh is not capable to handle this requirement, which I think it is a very big deficiency.  And even using JMS is very limited in regard of UX.
