If you work with the standard multiple users, are you satisfied with this?

Thanks,

 

Kristian

entry.title: Why Data Readiness Is the Biggest Success Factor in SAP S/4HANA Migrations?
entry.content:
What Does Data Readiness Mean in S/4HANA Migration? 

Data readiness refers to the process of preparing enterprise data so that it can be migrated into S/4HANA without introducing operational or reporting issues. 

This preparation typically includes several key activities. 

  1. Data profiling: Organizations analyze legacy datasets to identify duplicates, inconsistencies, and missing values. 
  2. Data cleansing: Incorrect or redundant records are corrected or removed to improve overall data quality. 
  3. Master data harmonization: Customer, vendor, and material records are standardized across business units. 
  4. Validation rule implementation: Business rules confirm that data behaves correctly within S/4HANA structures. 

These steps ensure that migrated data supports operational processes and financial reporting from the moment the system goes live. 

Why Does Poor Data Readiness Cause Migration Delays? 

Many S/4HANA programs encounter delays during testing cycles because legacy data structures are incompatible with the new system. 

Common problems include:

  • duplicate business partner records 
  • inconsistent product classifications 
  • incomplete historical transactions 
  • outdated financial mappings 

When these issues are discovered late in the project timeline, migration teams must pause testing cycles to correct underlying data problems. 

This remediation effort can extend migration timelines and increase project costs. 

Organizations that invest in early data readiness avoid these disruptions by identifying and correcting issues before migration testing begins. 

Which Data Areas Require the Most Preparation? 

Not all enterprise data carries the same level of migration risk. Certain datasets require particularly careful preparation. 

  1. Master Data: Customer, vendor, and material records must be standardized before migration to prevent duplicate or inconsistent entities in S/4HANA. 
  2. Financial Data: Chart-of-account mappings and financial structures must align with S/4HANA requirements to ensure accurate reporting. 
  3. Transactional Data: Open transactions, historical orders, and financial postings must be verified for completeness and accuracy. 
  4. Organizational Structures: Legacy system hierarchies must align with the simplified data model used by S/4HANA. 

Preparing these data domains early significantly improves migration stability. 

Common Data Readiness Challenges 

Organizations often underestimate the complexity of preparing legacy data for migration. 

Typical challenges include:

  • inconsistent data definitions across business units 
  • fragmented ownership of master data domains 
  • lack of automated validation rules 
  • limited visibility into data quality metrics 

Without structured governance frameworks, these challenges can delay migration progress. 

Enterprises increasingly recognize that data readiness is both a technical and organizational initiative. 

Data Readiness Challenges vs Prevention Strategies 

Organizations can mitigate migration risks by addressing common readiness challenges early. 

Data Readiness Challenge Operational Impact Prevention Strategy 
Duplicate master records Transaction errors Master data harmonization 
Incomplete transactional history Reporting gaps Data completeness validation 
Misaligned financial mappings Financial discrepancies Chart-of-account alignment 
Weak validation rules Data integrity issues Automated validation checks 
Fragmented ownership Slow issue resolution Governance framework 

These strategies help organizations move into S/4HANA with greater confidence. 

Case Illustration: Improving Data Readiness Before Migration 

A global manufacturing company preparing for S/4HANA migration encountered several issues during early testing cycles. Customer records were duplicated across regional systems, and inconsistent product classifications caused planning discrepancies. 

Initially, project teams attempted to correct these issues manually during testing cycles. However, the scale of the data environment made this approach inefficient. 

The organization implemented a structured data readiness program that included:

  • comprehensive data profiling across business units 
  • master data harmonization processes 
  • automated validation rules during testing cycles 

Governance-driven frameworks supported by solutions such as DataVapte helped automate validation checks, identify inconsistencies, and track data quality improvements. 

Within several testing cycles:

  • duplicate records were significantly reduced. 
  • reconciliation discrepancies were eliminated. 
  • migration testing stabilized. 

The organization completed its S/4HANA migration with minimal disruption.

How Can Enterprises Improve Data Readiness? 

Organizations preparing for S/4HANA migration can improve data readiness by implementing structured preparation strategies. 

  1. Begin Data Preparation Early: Data readiness initiatives should start well before migration testing begins. 
  2. Establish Data Ownership: Clear ownership ensures that responsible teams maintain data quality across domains. 
  3. Implement Automated Validation: Automated validation rules help detect inconsistencies quickly. 
  4. Monitor Data Quality Continuously: Regular monitoring ensures that improvements remain sustainable. 
  5. Adopt Governance-Driven Migration Tools: Modern governance frameworks—such as DataVapte, support validation, reconciliation, and exception management throughout the migration lifecycle. 

These capabilities help organizations maintain consistent data quality as they transition to S/4HANA. 

Why Data Readiness Enables Long-Term ERP Value 

Beyond migration success, strong data readiness practices provide long-term advantages. 

Organizations benefit from: 

  • improved reporting accuracy 
  • stronger analytics capabilities 
  • reduced operational errors 
  • better regulatory compliance 

Because S/4HANA enables real-time analytics and integrated business processes, maintaining high-quality data becomes even more important after migration. 

Data readiness therefore supports both immediate migration stability and long-term digital transformation. 

Conclusion 

S/4HANA migration programs often focus on infrastructure planning and implementation methodologies. Yet the factor that most frequently determines migration success is the readiness of enterprise data. 

Organizations that migrate legacy datasets without proper preparation risk introducing inconsistencies that disrupt operations and delay system stabilization. 

By investing in structured data readiness, through profiling, cleansing, validation, and governance, enterprises can significantly reduce migration risk and achieve a smoother transition to S/4HANA. 

Governance-driven solutions such as DataVapte further strengthen this process by helping organizations monitor data quality, automate validation checks, and maintain consistent data governance across the migration lifecycle. 

For more insights on SAP data governance and migration strategies, visit:
https://innovapte.com/insights

entry.title: Re: OVA8 - Days Open Items fields aging methodology
entry.content: The fields “NoDays OpenI” and “Days Oldest OpenI” in OVA8 do not count days from the posting date. They are based on the baseline date used for due date calculation. This means they measure days overdue (past due date) rather than days since posting. Open uncleared payment differences are also considered open items unless explicitly excluded by customizing or user exits.

entry.title: Re: External Sales Commission - Single Sales Deal
entry.content: Yes, an accrual account key is normally required, but in Public Cloud only certain predefined keys and condition types are settlement relevant. If RCD2 is not mapped to business volume relevance, settlement will never pick it up; regardless of account key. The next step is to check the settlement log and confirm whether RCD2 is supported; if not, you’ll need to use a delivered commission condition type or raise a request with SAP.

entry.title: Re: Cloud Connector installer interrupted
entry.content:
I've had the same issue just today when installing the newest version of Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 on Windows Server. 

The installer did not extract the com.sap.scc.zip-2.19.0.2-win64-msi.zip file (which is included in the .msi file) which leads to the error

Error: Unable to access jarfile configurator.jar ExecuteCommand(): Execution ended with return code 1.

In the com.sap.scc.zip-2.19.0.2-win64-msi.zip file is included the configurator.jar file - and of course, it cannot be accessed by the java command "java.exe -jar configurator.jar -upgrade" if it is not present unzipped in the installation folder. During installation, I manually extracted the zip-file into the installation directory (this should normally be done by the installer) and after that the installer ended successfully. 

Regards
Christian

entry.title: Isn't RAP overengineered ?
entry.content:
Isn't RAP totally over overengineered and almost turned into a tool for creating Rube Goldberg systems?

How would you make a small report in ABAP Cloud?

When I started programming ABAP I could make a report to list customers in 2 minutes like this.

REPORT ZLIST_CUSTOMERS.
Tables: kna1.
SELECT-OPTIONS s_mcod1 for kna1-mcod1.
select * from kna1
 where mcod1 in s_mcod1.
  write: / kna1-kunnr, kna1-name1.
endselect.

The users kun run the report from SA38 or I could spend additional 2 minutes to create a transaction code.

With RAP I could spend hours creating a RAP application for the same output and I have to create so many objects that I for sure will make some typing errors during the process. 

So now I need the help of Joule to create all the objects like this:

Joule create more objects than I used to have code lines in one object and all I get is still just a list of customers.
I know that RAP can make much more advanced apps, but often I don't need more.

On top of all this I probably have to create an additional bunch of objects to make the report appear in Build Workzone. 

Best regards 
Thomas Madsen Nielsen

entry.title: Re: Number Ranges for SD invoices
entry.content: In SAP SD, billing document number ranges are defined at the level of the billing document type, not directly per company code. Each billing type can only be assigned one number range object, and that object can contain multiple numeric intervals. Could you please confirm whether your requirement is to have separate number ranges per company code, or simply to ensure that each billing type used in those company codes issues invoices with 10‑digit numeric sequences (without letters)?

entry.title: Re: Production process mapping from Finished good (Varian...
entry.content:
Below are the recommended approaches

  • Step 1: Create production order for HRC_SCRAP against dummy scrap sales order.
  • Step 2: Assign HRC_F as BOM component in the order.
  • Step 3: Confirm production order (CO11N).
    • System consumes HRC_F (batch managed).
    • System produces HRC_SCRAP (non-batch managed).
    • Accounting entries:
      • Cr Inventory HRC_F (MAP).
      • Dr Inventory HRC_SCRAP (Standard).
      • Difference → PRD.
  • Step 4: Scrap material HRC_SCRAP is now available for SMS shop consumption.

entry.title: Public Cloud – Standard CDS for SII eDocument status/submission (Spain)

entry.title: Re: MIRO not calculating taxes (TAXBRA) – Invoice shows p...
entry.content:
Answer to your questions:

  1. Yes, this is related to missing LIV tax determination configuration.
  2. PO tax conditions (TAXBRA) must be linked to MIRO via tax code.
  3. The tax code should be copied automatically from the PO; manual entry is not expected.
  4. Brazil localization settings (CFOP, tax code, condition mapping) directly affect MIRO behavior.

Why this happens is because

1) PO vs. MIRO behavior:
In ME21N, TAXBRA determines taxes correctly based on CFOP, tax code, and condition records. In MIRO, the system expects the tax code to be carried over from the PO. If the tax code is missing or not copied, MIRO does not calculate taxes.

2) Gross amount entry:
Entering gross value in MIRO does not trigger tax determination. The system balances only if the tax code is present.

3) Brazil localization dependency:
TAXBRA requires correct CFOP, tax code, and condition setup. If the tax code is not mapped to LIV, MIRO will not propose taxes.

Key Configuration areas to check

1) Tax Code Copying
Ensure the tax code is maintained in the PO item and is copied into MIRO. In Brazil localization, the tax code is mandatory for LIV tax determination.

2) Condition Mapping
Verify that the PO tax conditions (ICMS, IPI, ISS, etc.) are linked to the tax code used in MIRO. Without this mapping, MIRO cannot calculate taxes.

3) LIV Settings
Check customizing in Logistics Invoice Verification → Incoming Invoice → Taxes. Confirm that the tax procedure TAXBRA is active for LIV and that the relevant tax codes are assigned.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Angel Aradhana, and I am excited to begin my journey on SAP.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Angel Aradhana , and I’m currently pursuing Mechanical Engineering at REVA University in Bangalore, India.

I am very happy to be part of the Hello SAP Community initiative. This opportunity allows beginners like me to explore the SAP ecosystem and connect with professionals around the world.

As a student, I am interested in learning new technologies and understanding how SAP solutions help businesses manage their operations efficiently. Through the SAP Community, I hope to learn from experts, discover new ideas, and grow my professional network.

I look forward to sharing my learning journey and contributing to the  SAP  Community in the future.

Thank you SAP Community for this wonderful opportunity!

 

 

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative.

1. What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to build a successful career in technology and engineering. I want to continuously learn new skills and work on innovative solutions that help organizations improve their efficiency. I also aim to contribute to the tech community and grow as a professional in the global technology ecosystem.

2. Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In the next five years, I see myself as a skilled professional in the technology industry. I aim to develop strong technical knowledge, gain hands-on experience, and contribute to real-world projects. I also aspire to continuously learn, grow professionally, and make a meaningful contribution to my organization and community

3. What is your hobby?

One of my hobbies is learning about new technologies and exploring new ideas related to engineering and innovation. I also enjoy reading, learning new skills online, and improving my knowledge through different educational platforms.

4. Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

There are several goals I want to achieve in my life:

Build a successful professional career.
Continuously learn and develop new skills.
Contribute to society through knowledge and innovation.
Inspire others through my journey and experiences.


5. What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The best lesson my parents taught me is the importance of hard work, honesty, and patience. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals and never give up during difficult situations. These values continue to guide me in my education and future career.

 

 

#HelloSAPcommunity #SAPcommunity #SAPLearning #SAPForBeginners #TechCommunity #FutureWithSAP

entry.title: Re: Mass download sales order form and invoice form
entry.content: One option is to use transaction RSNAST00 (classic output processing) or the equivalent in Output Management. You can schedule a job to process multiple output requests at once, generating PDFs for all selected documents. The PDFs can be stored in the SAP spool or sent to an external archive.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! I'm Pati Pradeep Babu
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community! I’m Pati Pradeep Babu, currently in my final year at Chalapathi Institute of Engineering and Technology. I still remember the first time I heard about SAP and realized it was the 'brain' behind almost every product I use daily. That realization sparked a deep passion in me for learning how these complex business processes work. As a newcomer, I’m excited to share my learning journey, connect with experts, and grow alongside this amazing community!

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the HelloSAPCommunity  initiative .

What is your professional life dream?  

My professional dream is to become a Certified SAP FICO Consultant at a globally recognized organization. Beyond just mastering the technical configurations of Finance and Controlling, I aim to be a bridge between complex business challenges and streamlined digital solutions. I want to help businesses achieve financial transparency and efficiency, ultimately contributing to the digital transformation of the industry while continuously evolving as an expert in the SAP ecosystem.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?  

The most realistic lesson I learned from my parents is that failure isn't the opposite of success; it’s a part of it. They never pressured me to be perfect, but they always insisted I 'get back up.' As a final-year student stepping into the professional world, that gives me the confidence to take on the steep learning curve of SAP without the fear of making mistakes because I know that’s how I’ll eventually become an expert.

What is my hobby?

Yoga: I’ve been practicing Hatha Yoga for years, and it’s become a huge part of my life—I’ve actually been fortunate enough to win 36 medals in various competitions! But for me, it’s not about the medals anymore; it’s about the mental discipline. In a field as complex as SAP, that ability to stay calm and focused is my secret weapon.

Swimming: I’ve always loved being in the water, and these days, I spend time as a Swimming Trainer. There is something incredibly rewarding about helping someone overcome their fear of water and master a new skill. It’s taught me a lot about patience and how to explain difficult things in simple ways.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore experience

I had the chance to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2026, and 'thrilling' is the only word that truly describes it. Being in a room filled with SAP experts, developers, and fellow students from all over was a massive eye-opener for me.

 

It wasn’t just about the sessions; it was about the energy of seeing how people exchange ideas and solve real-world problems. Seeing the latest advancements in AI and S/4HANA firsthand turned the concepts I’ve been studying into a living, breathing reality. It confirmed for me that I’m exactly where I’m supposed to be."

 
Main Highlights and SAP TechEd Key Takeaways
The event wasn't just about watching presentations; it was about the "aha!" moments that sharpened my practical understanding. Here are my key takeaways:
  • AI-Driven Roadmap: I attended immersive sessions on SAP’s AI-driven strategy and cloud innovations, which showed me how automation is transforming financial transparency.
  • Hands-on Labs: These sessions strengthened my technical skills in SAP integration and core business processes, moving me closer to my goal of professional certification.
  • Inspiring Connections: Having real conversations with the professionals who are driving digital transformation today boosted my confidence and motivated me to build smarter, sustainable enterprise solutions.
Walking out of SAP TechEd, I feel more empowered than ever to take the next steps in my tech journey. It wasn’t just an event—it was a turning point.
 
And now let’s hear from you!
I am excited to apply these insights and grow into a professional capable of building the future of enterprise technology. But before I go, I want to learn from this amazing community
 
"What was the one 'aha!' moment or specific event that started your own SAP journey?" Please share your stories in the comments section below!
